title: Model Outputs
text:
Population Trajectories
Each plot shows estimated adult abundance (blue line) with 50% CI (dark ribbon) and 90% CI (light ribbon) derived from the delta method on the ADREPORT-ed log-scale state. Open circles are observed redd counts scaled to adult-equivalents (redds ÷ 0.5 × survey coverage fraction), placing observations and model estimates on the same axis. The dashed red line marks the 20-adult management threshold.

title: Bull Trout Status and Threats
text:
Authors: Timothy Cline, Montana State University (corresponding author timothy.cline2@montana.edu) Clint Muhlfeld, USGS - Northern Rocky Mountain Science Center Robert Al-Chokhachy, USGS - Northern Rocky Mountain Science Center
Bull trout (Salvelinus confluentus) are a cold-water obligate salmonid native to western North America and listed as Threatened under the U.S. Endangered Species Act. This site presents a hierarchical state-space population model developed through a collaboration between the US Geological Survey, US Fish and Wildlife Service, Montana Fish Wildlife and Parks, and Montana State University. The goal is to synthesize redd count monitoring data across the Northern Rocky Mountains and assess population status, threats, and vulnerability to inform conservation strategies.

title: Bull Trout Status and Threats
text:
What this model does
The model integrates long-term redd count time series (1984–2022) from 80 local populations nested within 26 core areas to estimate:

Population-specific growth rates and how they vary with environmental conditions
Density dependence at the population level
Effects of climate (summer streamflow, August water temperature) on population growth
Biotic threats from non-native trout and lake trout
Habitat quality indicators (adfluvial life history access, valley bottom extent, W95 winter flood frequency, road density)

title: Bull Trout Status and Threats
text:
Site structure



Data
Explore the raw redd count time series and environmental covariate data for each core area and local population. Use the right-side table of contents to navigate directly to the populations under your purview.



Model Results
View fitted population trajectories, observed vs. fitted redd counts, covariate effect estimates (including population-specific random slopes), and model variance components.



About the model
The model is a hierarchical state-space Ricker model fitted with TMB (Template Model Builder). Process error follows an AR(1) structure; redd counts are modelled with a Negative Binomial (NB2) observation model to account for overdispersion. Where available, electrofishing CPUE data provides an independent abundance index via a catchability sub-model. Random effects are integrated out via the Laplace approximation. Parameter uncertainty is propagated via the delta method.

title: Bull Trout Status and Threats
text:
Species background
Bull trout require cold, clear, complex streams for spawning and rearing. They are possibly sensitive to:







Threat
Mechanism




Warming water temperatures
Upper thermal tolerance ~13–15 °C for spawning adults; lethal above ~20 °C


Altered hydrology
Peak flow timing and winter flood frequency affect gravel incubation success


Non-native trout
Competitive displacement and hybridization (brown trout, brook trout); predation (lake trout)


Road density
Fine sediment delivery to spawning gravels; barrier culverts


Lack or loss of migratory access
Adfluvial populations (lake-resident adults) have higher productivity




Model version 1.18 · Data version 1.5 · Source code at github.com/timothycline/BullTroutSSP

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis

# ── Load saved model fit ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fit <- readRDS(here("Results", paste0(SaveName, ".RDS")))
list2env(fit, envir = .GlobalEnv)

get_rep <- function(pname) {
  list(
    est = fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == pname],
    se  = fit_rep$sd[names(fit_rep$value)    == pname]
  )
}
re_summ <- summary(fit_rep, select = "random")
get_re  <- function(nm) unname(re_summ[rownames(re_summ) == nm, "Estimate"])
Show code
qext <- readRDS(here("Results", paste0(SaveName, "_Qext.RDS")))

nYears_proj <- qext$nYears_proj
proj_years  <- qext$proj_years
N_proj_arr  <- exp(qext$logN_proj)   # nsim x nPop x nYears_proj
nSim        <- dim(N_proj_arr)[1]

# Aggregate projected N to core level: sum populations within each core,
# then compute quantiles across simulations — gives proper uncertainty
# for the summed (core-level) abundance.
df_proj_core <- bind_rows(lapply(seq_len(nCore), function(cc) {
  pops   <- which(pop_core_vec == cc)
  Nc_sim <- if (length(pops) == 1)
               matrix(N_proj_arr[, pops, ], nrow = nSim)
             else
               apply(N_proj_arr[, pops, ], c(1, 3), sum)
  Nq <- apply(Nc_sim, 2, quantile,
               probs = c(0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.95), na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(core_name = U_CoreAreas_In[cc], year = proj_years,
             N_p05 = Nq[1,], N_p25 = Nq[2,], N_p50 = Nq[3,],
             N_p75 = Nq[4,], N_p95 = Nq[5,])
}))

# Per-population historical bands (lognormal from sdreport marginal SEs)
logN_map <- matrix(fit_rep$value[names(fit_rep$value) == "logN"],
                   nrow = nPop, ncol = data_list$nYears)
logN_sd  <- matrix(fit_rep$sd[  names(fit_rep$value) == "logN"],
                   nrow = nPop, ncol = data_list$nYears)
z90 <- qnorm(0.95); z50 <- qnorm(0.75)

df_hist_pop <- expand.grid(pop = seq_len(nPop),
                           y_idx = seq_len(data_list$nYears)) |>
  mutate(
    pop_name  = U_LocalPops_In[pop],
    core_name = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec[pop]],
    year      = DatYears[y_idx],
    N_p50     = exp(logN_map[cbind(pop, y_idx)]),
    N_p05     = exp(logN_map[cbind(pop, y_idx)] - z90 * logN_sd[cbind(pop, y_idx)]),
    N_p25     = exp(logN_map[cbind(pop, y_idx)] - z50 * logN_sd[cbind(pop, y_idx)]),
    N_p75     = exp(logN_map[cbind(pop, y_idx)] + z50 * logN_sd[cbind(pop, y_idx)]),
    N_p95     = exp(logN_map[cbind(pop, y_idx)] + z90 * logN_sd[cbind(pop, y_idx)])
  )

# Per-population projection bands (from simulation quantiles)
r1 <- qext$qext_results[["thr20"]]
df_proj_pop <- expand.grid(pop = seq_len(nPop),
                           h   = seq_len(nYears_proj)) |>
  mutate(
    pop_name  = U_LocalPops_In[pop],
    core_name = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec[pop]],
    year      = proj_years[h],
    N_p05     = r1$N_p05[cbind(pop, h)],
    N_p25     = r1$N_p25[cbind(pop, h)],
    N_p50     = r1$N_p50[cbind(pop, h)],
    N_p75     = r1$N_p75[cbind(pop, h)],
    N_p95     = r1$N_p95[cbind(pop, h)]
  )

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Model Description

Process Model
The latent population abundance follows a density-dependent Ricker process with AR(1) process error:
\[\log N_{p,t} = \log N_{p,t-1} + R_{p,t} + \varepsilon_{p,t}\]
where the instantaneous growth rate is:
\[R_{p,t} = b_{0r,p} + \beta_{\text{Flow},p} \cdot \text{Flow}_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{Temp},p} \cdot T_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{Temp}_2} \cdot T_{p,t-1}^2
           + \beta_{\text{INV},p} \cdot \text{INV}_{p,t-1}
           + \beta_{\text{LKT},c} \cdot \text{LKT}_{c,t-1}
           - \phi_p \cdot D_{p,t-1}\]
Process errors follow an AR(1) structure:
\[\varepsilon_{p,t} = \rho \cdot \varepsilon_{p,t-1} + \eta_{p,t}, \quad
  \eta_{p,t} \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\; \sigma_N \sqrt{1-\rho^2}\right)\]
All dynamic covariates carry an effective lag of 6 years (5-year R-side lag + 1-year C++ indexing), reflecting the time from in-gravel incubation to adult redd counts.


Observation Model
Redd counts at each survey site follow a Negative Binomial (NB2) distribution:
\[C_{s,y} \sim \text{NB2}\!\left(\mu_{s,y},\; \phi_{\text{obs}}\right), \quad
  \mu_{s,y} = 0.5 \cdot N_{p,y} \cdot \pi_s\]
where \(\phi_{\text{obs}}\) is the NB2 dispersion parameter (\(\text{Var} = \mu + \mu^2/\phi_{\text{obs}}\); as \(\phi_{\text{obs}} \to \infty\) the distribution collapses to Poisson), and \(\pi_s\) is the fraction of population habitat surveyed at site \(s\).


CPUE Sub-Model
For populations with electrofishing data, model-based CPUE (fish km⁻¹) provides an independent abundance index:
\[\log(\text{CPUE}_i) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\log N_{p,y} - \log(\text{extent}_p)
  + q_s,\; \sigma_{\text{CPUE}}\right)\]
where \(q_s\) is a section-level catchability random effect that absorbs persistent efficiency differences among electrofishing reaches.


Hierarchical Parameter Structure







Parameter
Hierarchy




Baseline growth rate \(b_{0r}\)
Global → Core → Population


Density dependence \(\phi\)
Global → Core → Population


Flow slope \(\beta_\text{Flow}\)
Global hyper-mean → Population


Temperature slope \(\beta_\text{Temp}\)
Global hyper-mean → Population


Invasive slope \(\beta_\text{INV}\)
Global hyper-mean → Population


Lake trout slope \(\beta_\text{LKT}\)
Global hyper-mean → Core area


Adfluvial access, unconfined valley bottom
Global scalar on \(b_{0r}\) mean


W95 winter flood frequency, road density
Global scalar on \(b_{0r}\) mean

Process Error and Observation Dispersion
AR(1) temporal autocorrelation and NB2 overdispersion absorb variance that would otherwise inflate covariate effects or process error. The AR(1) captures persistent multi-year trends in population dynamics; NB2 absorbs aggregated spawning behaviour and observer variability in redd counts.

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Covariate Effects

Static Effects on Baseline Growth Rate
These covariates shift the prior mean of the population-level growth rate intercept (\(b_{0r,p}\)). Adfluvial access (lake-associated populations) and unconfined valley bottom (floodplain habitat quality) reflect structural habitat attributes. W95 flood frequency and road density in the valley bottom capture disturbance.


Show code
global_pars <- c("bAdf", "bUVB", "bFlood", "bRD")

global_df <- lapply(global_pars, function(p) {
  r <- get_rep(p)
  if (!length(r$est)) return(NULL)
  data.frame(Parameter = p, Estimate = r$est[1], SE = r$se[1])
}) |>
  bind_rows() |>
  mutate(
    Label   = c("Adfluvial access",
                "Unconfined valley bottom (z)",
                "W95 winter flood frequency (z)",
                "Road density in valley bottom (z)"),
    Lower95 = Estimate - 1.96 * SE,
    Upper95 = Estimate + 1.96 * SE,
    sig     = ifelse(Lower95 > 0 | Upper95 < 0, "*", "")
  )

global_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Label, Estimate), y = Estimate)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Lower95, ymax = Upper95), width = 0.2, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_text(aes(label = sig, y = Upper95 + 0.01), size = 5, color = "tomato") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Coefficient estimate (95% CI)",
       title = "Static Covariate Effects on Baseline Growth Rate",
       caption = "* = 95% CI excludes zero") +
  theme_bw()











Thermal Performance Curve
Bull trout growth rate shows a unimodal response to water temperature (linear + negative quadratic).


Show code
if (covar_config$use_Temp == 1) {
  plot_temp_curve(fit_rep, temp_mean = temp_mean, temp_sd = temp_sd)
}


Thermal optimum (z-scored): -0.449 
Thermal optimum (raw scale): 9.39 











Population-Level Flow Slopes
Each local population has its own sensitivity to summer streamflow, drawn from a global hyper-distribution (dashed red line = hyper-mean).


Show code

  r <- get_rep("bFlow_pop")

                      ymax = estimate + 1.96 * se), width = 0.4, alpha = 0.6) +

}

    theme_bw(base_size = 9) +
    theme(legend.position = "right",
          axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7))
}











Global Lake Trout Slope
In model v1.22 the lake trout effect is a single global scalar \(\beta_{\text{LKT},M}\) applied to all core areas with confirmed lake trout presence (replacing the per-core random effects used in earlier versions).

  r <- get_rep("bLKT_M")
  data.frame(Parameter = "bLKT_M (global scalar)",
             Estimate  = round(r$est[1], 4),
             SE        = round(r$se[1],  4),
             Lower95   = round(r$est[1] - 1.96 * r$se[1], 4),
             Upper95   = round(r$est[1] + 1.96 * r$se[1], 4)) |>
    kable(col.names = c("Parameter", "Estimate", "SE", "Lower 95%", "Upper 95%")) |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
}

title: Bull Trout Multi-Population Viability Analysis
text:
Growth Rate and Density Dependence

Baseline Growth Rates by Population


Show code
b0r <- get_rep("b0r_pop")
b0r_df <- data.frame(
  pop_name  = U_LocalPops_In,
  core_name = U_CoreAreas_In[pop_core_vec],
  b0r       = b0r$est,
  b0r_se    = b0r$se
)

ggplot(b0r_df, aes(x = reorder(pop_name, b0r), y = b0r, color = core_name)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = b0r - 1.96 * b0r_se,
                    ymax = b0r + 1.96 * b0r_se), width = 0.4, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 1.8) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set3", name = "Core area") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Baseline growth rate (b0r_pop)",
       title = "Population-Level Baseline Growth Rates",
       caption = "Positive = growing at mean covariate conditions; negative = declining") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 9) +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7))











Variance Components


Show code
sigma_pars <- c(
  "sigmaN"          = "Process error σ_N (innovation)",
  "sigma_b0r_core"  = "Growth rate: core-level SD",
  "sigma_b0r_pop"   = "Growth rate: pop-level SD",
  "sigma_b0phi_pop" = "Density dependence: pop SD",
  "sigma_bFlow"     = "Flow slope: pop SD",
  "sigma_bTemp"     = "Temp slope: pop SD",
  "sigma_bINV"      = "Invasive slope: pop SD",
  "sigma_bLKT"      = "Lake trout slope: core SD",
  "sigma_cpue"      = "CPUE observation SD",
  "sigma_q"         = "CPUE catchability SD"
)

sigma_df <- lapply(names(sigma_pars), function(nm) {
  r <- get_rep(nm)
  if (!length(r$est)) return(NULL)
  data.frame(Label = sigma_pars[nm], Estimate = r$est[1], SE = r$se[1])
}) |>
  bind_rows()

sigma_df |>
  mutate(Lower95 = pmax(0, Estimate - 1.96 * SE),
         Upper95 = Estimate + 1.96 * SE) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Label, Estimate), y = Estimate)) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = Lower95, ymax = Upper95), width = 0.25, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Standard deviation estimate (95% CI)",
       title = "Variance Component Estimates") +
  theme_bw()

  geom_ribbon(data = df_hist_pop,
              aes(x = year, ymin = N_p05, ymax = N_p95),
              fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_ribbon(data = df_hist_pop,
              aes(x = year, ymin = N_p25, ymax = N_p75),
              fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.30) +
  geom_line(data = df_hist_pop,
            aes(x = year, y = N_p50),
            color = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = DatYears[data_list$nYears] + 0.5,
             linetype = 2, color = "grey50", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_ribbon(data = df_proj_pop,
              aes(x = year, ymin = N_p05, ymax = N_p95),
              fill = "tomato", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_ribbon(data = df_proj_pop,
              aes(x = year, ymin = N_p25, ymax = N_p75),
              fill = "tomato", alpha = 0.30) +
  geom_line(data = df_proj_pop,
            aes(x = year, y = N_p50),
            color = "tomato", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 20, linetype = 3,
             color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.4) +
  facet_wrap(~pop_name, scales = "free_y", ncol = 5) +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Adult Abundance (N)",
       title = "Local Population Abundance - Historical and Projected") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 7) +
  theme(strip.text      = element_text(size = 5),
        axis.text.x     = element_text(size = 5, angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
